title: Spring Core - IoC & DI
learningObjectives: Explain Spring Core - IoC & DI with phase-5 depth and clarity
Implement Java examples for Spring Core - IoC & DI with production awareness
Identify failure risks and mitigation strategies
Reason about trade-offs under realistic constraints
Design or optimize solutions with maintainability in mind
Present interview answers with concrete evidence and structure

sections.title: Why Spring Core - IoC & DI matters
sections.content:
Spring Core - IoC & DI is a high-value Day 38 topic in Phase 5 (Spring ecosystem and enterprise backend patterns).

At this stage, interviewers evaluate whether you can connect implementation details with reliability, scalability, and maintainability outcomes.

What strong answers include:
1. precise concept framing,
2. operational implications,
3. one realistic failure pattern,
4. one practical mitigation.

This topic is not just about code that compiles. It is about decisions that survive production traffic and team ownership.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Spring Core - IoC & DI
sections.content:
### Day 38: Spring Core - IoC & DI
Phase context: Spring ecosystem and enterprise backend patterns.

### What to understand deeply
- mechanism and boundaries
- runtime behavior and cost
- operational failure modes
- trade-offs vs alternatives

### Implementation workflow
1. Build a clear baseline solution.
2. Validate edge/failure paths.
3. Measure impact (latency, memory, throughput, or complexity).
4. Refactor for readability and maintainability.

### Interview framing template
- "The problem this solves is..."
- "I chose this approach because..."
- "The key trade-off is..."
- "A failure case is... and mitigation is..."

### Production perspective
For Spring Core - IoC & DI, quality is judged by correctness + resilience + debuggability, not just API usage.

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**Spring Core:** IoC container owns object graphs — prefer constructor injection for required dependencies; `@Autowired` field injection hides test pain.

**Bean scopes:** singleton default; prototype when stateful; request/session in web apps — wrong scope causes subtle bugs.

**`@Configuration` + `@Bean`** vs component scanning — explicit beans for third-party types.

**Interview:** explain lifecycle: instantiate → populate → pre-init → init → ready → destroy callbacks.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: Manual constructor injection (Spring-style idea without framework): depend on interface.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Simple service locator map: string key -> bean (mini IoC container).

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: Decorator pattern: wrap a bean to add cross-cutting behavior (AOP-like).

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Relying on Spring defaults without understanding lifecycle and bean scope.
Leaking transaction boundaries across service methods.
Ignoring lazy loading and query shape in persistence logic.
Implementing security rules without end-to-end flow clarity.
Using framework annotations mechanically without design intent.

sections.title: Exercise - Spring Core - IoC & DI Applied Scenario
sections.problem:
Implement a realistic solution for **Spring Core - IoC & DI**.

Requirements:
1. Define assumptions and constraints first.
2. Include one failure-path handling strategy.
3. Provide one improvement over baseline.
4. State complexity or runtime impact.
5. Keep implementation review-friendly.
hints: Add one observability/debug aid in output or comments.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is Inversion of Control?
conceptual.answer: Framework controls flow and calls your code—contrasts with library you call.

conceptual.question: What is Dependency Injection?
conceptual.answer: Dependencies provided from outside (constructor/setter) rather than created internally—testability.

conceptual.question: What is Spring ApplicationContext?
conceptual.answer: IoC container managing bean lifecycle and wiring.

conceptual.question: What is @Autowired?
conceptual.answer: Injection by type (and qualifier)—constructor injection preferred for immutability.

conceptual.question: What is @Component @Service @Repository?
conceptual.answer: Stereotypes marking Spring beans—semantics and AOP proxies differ slightly.

conceptual.question: What is bean scope singleton default?
conceptual.answer: One instance per container—stateless services ideal.

conceptual.question: What is @Primary @Qualifier?
conceptual.answer: Disambiguate multiple beans of same type.

conceptual.question: What is circular dependency problem?
conceptual.answer: Bean A needs B needs A—constructor injection fails; refactor or setter injection cautiously.

conceptual.question: What is ApplicationContext vs BeanFactory?
conceptual.answer: ApplicationContext superset—events, i18n, eager init of singletons by default.

conceptual.question: What is stereotype component scanning?
conceptual.answer: Classpath scanning for annotated classes—@SpringBootApplication includes.

conceptual.question: What is Spring profiles?
conceptual.answer: Beans activated per environment—dev, test, prod.

conceptual.question: What is conditional beans @Conditional?
conceptual.answer: Register beans based on classpath, properties, etc.

conceptual.question: What is FactoryBean?
conceptual.answer: Advanced indirection creating complex beans—rare interview depth.

codeBased.question: ApplicationContext getBean usage—when?
codeBased.answer: Generally avoid in application code—prefer injection.

seniorScenario.question: Huge Spring context slow startup?
seniorScenario.answer: Lazy beans, trimming classpath, spring-native/AOT strategies.

seniorScenario.question: Wrong bean in prod—debug?
seniorScenario.answer: Actuator beans endpoint, conditional report, logging.

seniorScenario.question: Testing without Spring context—approach?
seniorScenario.answer: Pure unit tests with manual mocks vs @SpringBootTest slices.

seniorScenario.question: Microservices shared library Spring configs conflict?
Memory leak Spring singleton holding HTTP session?
seniorScenario.answer: Design stateless services; scope session carefully.

wrongAnswers: @Autowired always injects by name first, never by type.
Spring beans are always prototype scope unless specified.
You should call new on @Service classes in your code.
IoC means objects create themselves more easily.
ApplicationContext and BeanFactory are identical in features.
Constructor injection cannot be used with final fields.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Phase 5 Expectation | Quick Rule | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Framing | Explain intent + constraints | Start from problem | API-only answer |
| Design | State trade-off explicitly | Compare alternatives | One-solution bias |
| Runtime | Mention cost impact | Time + space/ops | No performance reasoning |
| Failure | Name one incident pattern | Mitigation path | Happy-path only thinking |
| Communication | Be concise + concrete | Use one example | Generic buzzwords |
